name: Amravati
description: Amravati, also known as Amaravati, is the capital city of Indra, the king of the gods (Devas), in Hindu mythology. It is situated in Svarga (heaven) and is renowned for its opulence, beauty, and divine nature. Amravati is described as a celestial city filled with magnificent palaces, gardens, and celestial beings. It's a place of eternal bliss and abundance, where the gods reside in splendor. The city is often depicted as being adorned with precious jewels, gold, and divine architecture, reflecting the supreme power and prosperity of Indra. Entrance to Amravati is generally reserved for those who have performed great deeds, lived virtuous lives, or sacrificed themselves for a noble cause. The concept of Amravati represents the ultimate reward for righteous actions and is a symbol of divine grace and eternal happiness. Its significance lies in its portrayal of a perfect world governed by divine principles, serving as an aspirational goal for devotees seeking spiritual fulfillment. The city is often mentioned in various Hindu scriptures, including the Puranas and the epics, highlighting its importance in the Hindu cosmology.
subtitle: The Celestial Abode of Indra

keyMyths.title: Indra's Reign
keyMyths.description: Indra rules Amravati with unparalleled power and maintains order in the heavens. His role is crucial in balancing the cosmic forces and ensuring the prosperity of the gods.

keyMyths.title: Arjuna's Visit
keyMyths.description: In the Mahabharata, Arjuna visits Amravati and receives divine weapons from Indra. This visit strengthens Arjuna's abilities for the upcoming Kurukshetra war.

sources.author: Veda Vyasa
sources.description: An epic narrative containing numerous references to Amravati and Indra's court.
sources.text: An epic narrative containing numerous references to Amravati and Indra's court.

sources.author: Various Authors
sources.description: A collection of ancient texts that describe the Hindu cosmology, including the celestial realm of Amravati.
